title: Today's AI Brief: Open-Source Coding Models and AI Psychosis
summary: A look at Nous Research's new open-source coding model and the debate over AI psychosis
imageAlt: Image from source article for Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment
imageCaption: Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment
imageSourceTitle: Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. We're covering some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Nous Research has just released an open-source coding model called NousCoder-14B. This new model is making waves in the coding community, and for good reason.

NousCoder-14B is a competitive programming model that's said to match or exceed several larger proprietary models. What's significant here is that Nous Research is an open-source AI startup backed by crypto venture firm Paradigm. This move could potentially shake up the coding model landscape, making high-quality coding tools more accessible to developers.

Next, let's talk about AI psychosis. Box founder Aaron Levie recently sparked a conversation about tech CEOs being 'uniquely prone to AI psychosis.' This term refers to a kind of overconfidence or delusion about the capabilities of AI. It's an interesting debate, and one that highlights the complexities of working with AI systems that are increasingly sophisticated.

The release of NousCoder-14B lands right in the midst of the Claude Code moment, which suggests that we're in a period of rapid progress for coding models. My read is that this is a sign of how quickly the economics of AI model development are shifting. With more open-source models emerging, the dynamics of the market are likely to change in interesting ways.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
